question: Which HTML5 element is the correct semantic choice for the main navigation links of a page?
explanation: <nav> is the HTML5 landmark element for navigation links. It communicates intent to browsers, screen readers, and search engines, unlike a generic <div>.

question: What is the correct HTML5 doctype declaration?
explanation: The HTML5 doctype is <!DOCTYPE html> (case-insensitive). It signals standards mode without the verbose SGML identifiers required by HTML 4.01.

question: What does `<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">` do?
options: Sets the window width to the physical screen resolution
Tells the browser to match viewport width to device CSS width and start at 1:1 zoom
explanation: This tag instructs mobile browsers to set viewport width to the device's CSS pixel width and start with 1:1 zoom, enabling proper responsive design.

question: Which input type provides built-in email format validation and a specialized mobile keyboard?
explanation: <input type="email"> validates email format, shows an @ keyboard on mobile, and communicates intent to assistive technologies.

question: When should the alt attribute of an be set to an empty string (alt="")?
options: When the image is purely decorative and conveys no information
When the image is very small
Never — alt must always contain descriptive text
explanation: Decorative images should have alt="" so screen readers skip them. A missing alt attribute is a violation; an intentionally empty alt is not.

question: What is the difference between defer and async on a <script> tag?
options: defer executes after DOM parsing; async executes as soon as downloaded, possibly before DOM is ready
async executes after DOM parsing; defer executes immediately
defer is for ES modules only
explanation: async downloads and executes immediately, potentially interrupting parsing. defer downloads in parallel and executes after HTML is fully parsed, in document order.

question: How do you read data-user-id="42" in JavaScript?
explanation: The dataset API converts kebab-case to camelCase. data-user-id → element.dataset.userId. element.getAttribute('data-user-id') also works but dataset is cleaner.

question: What is the purpose of the srcset attribute on an ?
options: Provides fallback URLs if an image fails to load
Offers candidate images at different sizes so the browser picks the best
Defines multiple formats for cross-browser support
explanation: srcset gives the browser candidate images with width or pixel-density descriptors. The browser selects the most appropriate based on device resolution and viewport size.

question: Where must the charset meta tag appear in an HTML document?
options: Within the first 1024 bytes, inside <head> and before <title>
explanation: The charset declaration must appear within the first 1024 bytes so the browser detects encoding before parsing any content. It must be the first element inside <head>, before <title>.

question: What does tabindex="-1" do to an element?
options: Removes the element from the page
Places it last in the tab order
Allows programmatic focus while removing it from the sequential tab order
Makes it the first focusable element
explanation: tabindex="-1" removes an element from natural tab order but allows focus via element.focus(). Useful for programmatic focus management in modals and custom widgets.

question: When should aria-label be used instead of aria-labelledby?
options: When the label text is visible on screen
When there is no visible text element to reference
When the element is a landmark
explanation: aria-labelledby references an existing visible element (preferred when visible text exists). aria-label provides an inline string when no visible label text is present on the page.

question: What is the purpose of <link rel="canonical" href="...">?
options: Tells search engines the preferred URL when duplicate content exists
explanation: The canonical hint tells crawlers which URL to index when the same content is reachable from multiple URLs, preventing duplicate content penalties.

question: What does the scope attribute on a <th> element do?
options: Sets CSS scope for table styles
Tells screen readers whether the header applies to a column or row
explanation: scope="col" or scope="row" on <th> associates it with the cells it labels. Screen readers announce the correct header when users navigate each data cell.

question: What is the difference between rel="preload" and rel="prefetch"?
options: preload fetches a critical current-page resource at high priority; prefetch fetches a future-navigation resource at low priority
prefetch fetches current-page resources; preload is for future pages
preload is for CSS only; prefetch is for JavaScript
explanation: preload is a high-priority hint for resources needed NOW (critical font, hero image). prefetch is low-priority for resources likely needed AFTER navigation.

question: Which HTML element holds inert, reusable markup that is parsed but not rendered?
explanation: <template> content is parsed but not rendered. Its content is a DocumentFragment accessible via element.content, used as the basis for Web Component stamping.

question: Which statement about <script type="module"> is correct?
options: Module scripts block HTML parsing synchronously
Module scripts are deferred and run in strict mode automatically
Module scripts share the global scope with classic scripts
Module scripts cannot use top-level await
explanation: ES module scripts are deferred by default, run in strict mode, have their own module scope, and support top-level await.

question: Which autocomplete token should be used on a credit card number field?
explanation: The HTML spec defines specific tokens: cc-number for card number, cc-name, cc-exp-month, cc-exp-year, cc-csc. These enable correct autofill across browsers and password managers.

question: How can a form input physically outside a <form> element be associated with it?
options: Impossible — inputs must be inside the form
By setting the input's form attribute to the form's id
By using JavaScript to push it into form.elements
explanation: The form attribute on any form-associated element accepts a form's id, associating it regardless of DOM position — enabling flexible layouts with controls outside the form element.

question: What does aria-live="polite" communicate to screen readers?
options: Announce updates when the user is idle, without interrupting current speech
Immediately interrupt and announce any change
Hide the region from screen readers
explanation: aria-live="polite" queues announcements for after the current utterance — for status messages. aria-live="assertive" interrupts immediately — for critical errors.

question: What advantage does with have over alone?
options: <picture> enables different image compositions per breakpoint (art direction); srcset only changes resolution
<picture> works without JavaScript; srcset requires it
explanation: with provides art direction — completely different crops per viewport. srcset on only helps choose the best resolution of the same image.

question: Which statement about heading hierarchy best reflects current HTML and SEO guidance?
options: Multiple <h1> per page is always correct in HTML5
One <h1> per page is strongly recommended; headings must not skip levels
Headings have no impact on SEO or accessibility
The <h1> must exactly match the <title> element
explanation: The HTML5 outline algorithm was never browser-implemented and was removed from the spec. One h1 per page is best practice for SEO. Skipping heading levels (h1→h3) breaks the navigable outline for screen reader users.

question: What does the <slot> element do inside a Web Component's Shadow DOM?
options: Defines an insertion point where Light DOM children are projected into the Shadow DOM
Creates a form input inside the shadow root
Imports styles from the outer document
explanation: <slot> is the composition mechanism in Shadow DOM. Light DOM children matching the slot's name are projected at that position. The unnamed default slot catches all unassigned children.

question: Which of the following are HTML5 sectioning content elements?
explanation: <article>, <section>, <aside>, and <nav> are HTML5 sectioning content elements. <div> is a generic container with no semantic meaning.

question: Which elements belong inside <head>?
Which input types provide built-in validation or specialized mobile keyboards?
explanation: type="email" validates format. type="tel" shows a numeric keypad. type="number" restricts to numbers. type="text" has no special behavior. type="hidden" is non-interactive.

question: Which HTML elements and attributes directly influence SEO?
explanation: Title, meta description (affects CTR), headings (structure signals), and alt text (image indexing) all impact SEO. <meta name="keywords"> has been ignored by Google since the early 2000s.

question: Which of the following improve accessibility in a form with multiple groups of related inputs?
options: Replacing all <label> elements with placeholder attributes
Adding aria-labelledby on each input group
explanation: <fieldset> + <legend> group related controls with a visible title that screen readers announce. aria-labelledby on the group also works. placeholder is not a substitute for <label> — it disappears on input and has low contrast.

question: Which attributes can appear on a <source> inside a <picture>?
explanation: inside accepts: srcset, media, type, and sizes. alt belongs only on the fallback — not on .

question: Which statements about <script type="module"> are correct?
options: Module scripts are deferred by default
Module scripts run in strict mode
Module scripts share the global scope with classic scripts
A module URL is fetched and executed only once
explanation: ES modules: are deferred (A), strict mode (B), support top-level await (C), cached/executed once (E). They do NOT share the global scope — each module has its own scope (D is false).

question: Which ARIA roles are redundant because a native HTML element equivalent exists?
explanation: role="button" duplicates <button>, role="navigation" duplicates <nav>, role="list" duplicates <ul>/<ol>. Native elements include built-in keyboard behavior. role="tooltip" and role="banner" have no single direct HTML equivalent.

question: Which statements about <header> and <footer> elements are correct?
options: A page may contain multiple <header> and <footer> elements
There can be only one <header> per page
<footer> inside <article> represents the article's footer, not the page footer
explanation: <header> and <footer> are sectioning elements, not unique landmarks. Each <article> or <section> can have its own <header> and <footer>. <header> frequently contains <nav>. There is no one-per-page restriction.

question: Which HTML attributes enable built-in form field constraint validation?
explanation: required, pattern, and min/max participate in the HTML5 constraint validation API and prevent form submission on failure. maxlength limits input length but doesn't add a validation message. placeholder is purely presentational.

question: Which techniques improve the accessibility of a data table?
options: Using the headers attribute on <td> referencing <th> ids
explanation: <caption> titles the table, scope associates headers with cells, thead/tbody/tfoot provide structure, and headers handles complex multi-level tables. role="presentation" removes all table semantics — making data tables inaccessible.

question: Which statements about ARIA roles and keyboard interaction are correct?
options: Adding role="button" to a <div> automatically adds Enter/Space keyboard handling
Using native <button> is preferred over role="button" on a <div>
aria-disabled="true" removes the element from the tab order
aria-expanded is appropriate for disclosure buttons
role="tab" requires managing focus with arrow keys per ARIA APG
explanation: ARIA roles convey semantics but not behavior. role="button" on a div does NOT add keyboard handling (A false). aria-disabled does not remove from tab order — only signals state (C false). Native <button> is always preferred (B), aria-expanded describes state (D), tab pattern requires arrow key management (E).

question: Find the accessibility bug in this form snippet:
correct: The <label> is missing the for attribute pointing to the input's id
referenceAnswer: Fix: <label for="email">Email</label>. Without the for/id association, clicking the label does not focus the input and screen readers cannot announce the label when the input receives focus.
explanation: A <label> must be associated with its control via for/id pairing (or by wrapping the input). Without association, click-to-focus fails and screen readers skip the label.

question: Find the structural issue in this image markup:
correct: By convention,
referenceAnswer: Reorder: . Image first, caption second matches visual and reading order.
explanation: <figcaption> can be first or last child of <figure>. Convention places it after the image to match the reading flow where image precedes caption.

question: Find the bug in this HTML head:
correct: The charset meta must appear before <title>. Non-ASCII characters in the title require charset to be declared first.
referenceAnswer: Move <meta charset="UTF-8"> before <title>: <head><meta charset="UTF-8"><title>My Page — Привет</title></head>
explanation: Browsers parse before downloading the full document. Non-ASCII characters in <title> need the charset to be processed first. The spec requires charset within the first 1024 bytes, before <title>.

question: Find the security issue in this third-party iframe:
correct: The iframe is missing the sandbox attribute, giving embedded content full script execution, form submission, and navigation permissions
referenceAnswer: Add sandbox="allow-scripts allow-same-origin" (or minimum required tokens). Without sandbox, third-party content can run scripts, access cookies, navigate the top frame, and submit forms.
explanation: Third-party iframes should always have sandbox for least-privilege containment. Grant only the minimum tokens the specific widget requires.

question: Find the structural bug in this table:
correct: The second row has 3 cells but the header defines only 2 columns
referenceAnswer: Remove Extra cell, or add a matching in the header row. Tables require consistent cell counts per row (or explicit colspan/rowspan).
explanation: Mismatched column counts break visual alignment and confuse screen readers that navigate tables by column. Each row must match the column count.

question: Find the bug: the select should show 'Choose a country' as a placeholder that cannot be submitted:
code:
<select name="country">
  <option value="">Choose a country</option>
  <option value="uk">United Kingdom</option>
  <option value="de">Germany</option>
</select>
correct: The placeholder option is missing the disabled attribute — users can submit the form with an empty country value
referenceAnswer: Add disabled to the placeholder option: <option value="" disabled>Choose a country</option>. Also consider selected to show it initially: <option value="" disabled selected>Choose a country</option>
explanation: A placeholder <option> with an empty value but without disabled allows submitting a blank choice. Adding disabled prevents selection while still showing it as the default visible option.

question: Find the critical bug in this responsive image markup:
correct: The element is missing the mandatory fallback element
referenceAnswer: Add as the last child of . Without it, unsupported formats and browsers render nothing.
explanation: requires an as its last child. serves as the fallback and carries the alt, loading, and decoding attributes.

question: Find the accessibility bugs in this custom interactive element:
correct: Missing tabindex="0" (not keyboard focusable) and missing keyboard handlers for Enter and Space keys
referenceAnswer: Add tabindex="0" and onkeydown handling Enter and Space. Better: replace with a real <button> element which provides all this built-in.
explanation: role="button" grants ARIA semantics but not behavior. You must add tabindex="0" and keyboard event listeners. Using <button> is always preferred.

question: Complete the accessible navigation landmark:
referenceAnswer: <nav aria-label="Main navigation"> creates a navigation landmark. aria-label differentiates it from other nav elements on the same page.
explanation: <nav> is the correct landmark for navigation. When multiple nav elements exist, aria-label differentiates them for screen reader landmark navigation.

question: Complete the accessible, validated email input:
referenceAnswer: for="user-email" associates the label. id="user-email" matches the for. required enables browser constraint validation.
explanation: Accessible forms need explicit label/input association via for/id. The required attribute enables built-in validation preventing empty submissions.

question: Complete the minimal valid HTML5 document structure:
referenceAnswer: <!DOCTYPE html> declares the HTML5 doctype. <head> contains metadata. The closing </head> ends the head section before <body>.
explanation: A valid HTML5 document requires the DOCTYPE declaration, html element with lang, head section for metadata, and body for content.

question: Complete the responsive image with AVIF and WebP sources:
referenceAnswer: srcset on <source> specifies the image URL. type="image/avif" declares MIME type for format negotiation. loading="lazy" enables native lazy loading.
explanation: uses srcset (not src). The type attribute enables format negotiation — browser picks the first it supports. loading="lazy" on defers loading until near the viewport.

question: Complete the JavaScript reading and writing data attributes:
referenceAnswer: btn.dataset.itemId reads data-item-id. btn.dataset.itemCount = '5' writes data-item-count. The dataset API converts kebab-case to camelCase.
explanation: HTMLElement.dataset provides a DOMStringMap with camelCase access to data-* attributes. Writing to dataset also updates the HTML attribute.

question: Complete the accessible disclosure button:
code: Extra info.
referenceAnswer: aria-controls="details" links to the controlled element. aria-expanded="false" signals the collapsed state. hidden hides the div from both visual display and screen readers.
explanation: Disclosure widgets need: aria-controls (association), aria-expanded (state), and hidden on the content (so screen readers also see it as hidden, not just CSS-hidden).

question: Complete the accessible data table:
referenceAnswer: <caption> titles the table programmatically. scope="col" on <th> associates it with the column's data cells.
explanation: <caption> is the preferred way to title a table — programmatically associated. scope="col" on <th> enables correct screen reader header announcement during table navigation.

question: Complete the custom element with correct lifecycle hooks:
referenceAnswer: observedAttributes is a static getter listing attributes to monitor for attributeChangedCallback. connectedCallback fires when the element is inserted into the document.
explanation: observedAttributes must list attribute names for attributeChangedCallback to fire. connectedCallback is called when the element is connected to a document — the most common lifecycle hook for initial rendering.
